steps.prompt: How many main finite verbs are in this sentence?

hints.one: 'oportebat': 'Scriptum est' is also a compound finite verb (perfect passive)
hints.three: 'Pati' and 'resurgere' are infinitives, not finite verbs — there are exactly two
hints.one: 'est': 'Est' forms a compound with 'scriptum', and 'oportebat' is also finite

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'scriptum est' translates to?

hints.to write: 'To write' is active 'scribere' — 'scriptum est' is perfect passive

steps.prompt: What tense and mood is 'scriptum est'?
steps.reveal: scriptum est = it has been written / it is written (3rd sing. perf. pass.)

hints.present passive indicative: Present passive would be 'scribitur' — 'scriptum est' is perfect
hints.pluperfect passive indicative: Pluperfect passive would be 'scriptum erat' — 'scriptum est' is perfect
hints.perfect passive subjunctive: Perfect passive subjunctive would be 'scriptum sit' — 'scriptum est' is indicative

hints.sculpo, sculpere, sculpsi, sculptum: These are the parts of 'sculpo' (to carve)
hints.scio, scire, scivi, scitum: These are the parts of 'scio' (to know)
hints.seco, secare, secui, sectum: These are the parts of 'seco' (to cut)

steps.reveal: oportebat = it was necessary (3rd sing. impf., impersonal)

hints.imperfect subjunctive: Imperfect subjunctive would be 'oporteret' — 'oportebat' with -bat is indicative
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'oportuit' — 'oportebat' with -bat is imperfect

hints.operor, operari, operatus sum, —: These are the parts of 'operor' (to work)
hints.opto, optare, optavi, optatum: These are the parts of 'opto' (to wish)
hints.obtineo, obtinere, obtinui, obtentum: These are the parts of 'obtineo' (to hold/obtain)

steps.prompt: What is special about 'pati' as an infinitive?
steps.target: it is a deponent verb (passive form, active meaning: 'to suffer')
choices: it is a deponent verb (passive form, active meaning: 'to suffer')
it is a regular active infinitive
it is a passive infinitive of a regular verb
steps.reveal: pati = to suffer (deponent: patior, pati, passus sum)

hints.it is a regular active infinitive: Active infinitive of 3rd conjugation ends in -ere — 'pati' has passive form but active meaning
hints.it is a passive infinitive of a regular: 'Pati' is from a deponent verb, not a regular passive
hints.it is a gerund: A gerund would be 'patiendum' — 'pati' is the deponent infinitive

hints.pando, pandere, pandi, passum: These are the parts of 'pando' (to spread) — note similar 4th part
hints.pasco, pascere, pavi, pastum: These are the parts of 'pasco' (to feed)
hints.pateo, patere, patui, —: These are the parts of 'pateo' (to lie open/be evident)

hints.to destroy: 'To destroy' is 'destruere' — 'resurgere' means 'to rise again' (re- + surgere)

hints.surgo, surgere, surrexi, surrectum: These are the parts of 'surgo' (to rise) — 'resurgere' adds re- (again)
hints.rego, regere, rexi, rectum: These are the parts of 'rego' (to rule)
hints.redeo, redire, redii, reditum: These are the parts of 'redeo' (to go back)

steps.target: accusative — subject of the infinitives 'pati' and 'resurgere' after 'oportebat'
choices: accusative — subject of the infinitives 'pati' and 'resurgere' after 'oportebat'
steps.reveal: oportebat Christum pati et resurgere = it was necessary for Christ to suffer and rise

hints.accusative — direct object of 'oportebat: 'Oportebat' is impersonal — 'Christum' is the subject of the infinitive, not the object of the impersonal verb
hints.nominative — subject of 'oportebat': 'Christum' with -um is accusative — 'oportebat' is impersonal (no nominative subject)
hints.ablative — agent: Ablative of agent would be 'a Christo' — 'Christum' is accusative subject of the infinitive

translation: Thus it is written and thus it was necessary for Christ to suffer and to rise again
